speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Here art thou in appointment fresh and fair,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Anticipating time with starting courage.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Give with thy trumpet a loud note to Troy,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Thou dreadful Ajax, that the appallèd air

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: May pierce the head of the great combatant

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: And hale him hither.

speaker: AJAX
text: Thou, trumpet, there’s my purse.

speaker: AJAX
text: Now crack thy lungs and split thy brazen pipe.

speaker: AJAX
text: Blow, villain, till thy spherèd bias cheek

speaker: AJAX
text: Outswell the colic of puffed Aquilon.

speaker: AJAX
text: Come, stretch thy chest, and let thy eyes spout blood.

speaker: AJAX
text: Thou blowest for Hector.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: No trumpet answers.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: ’Tis but early days.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Is not yond Diomed with Calchas’ daughter?

speaker: ULYSSES
text: ’Tis he. I ken the manner of his gait.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: He rises on the toe; that spirit of his

speaker: ULYSSES
text: In aspiration lifts him from the earth.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Is this the Lady Cressid?

speaker: DIOMEDES
text: Even she.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Most dearly welcome to the Greeks, sweet lady.

speaker: NESTOR
text: Our general doth salute you with a kiss.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Yet is the kindness but particular.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: ’Twere better she were kissed in general.

speaker: NESTOR
text: And very courtly counsel. I’ll begin.

speaker: NESTOR
text: So much for Nestor.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: I’ll take that winter from your lips, fair lady.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Achilles bids you welcome.

speaker: MENELAUS
text: I had good argument for kissing once.

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: But that’s no argument for kissing now,

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: For thus popped Paris in his hardiment

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: And parted thus you and your argument.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: O deadly gall and theme of all our scorns,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: For which we lose our heads to gild his horns!

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: The first was Menelaus’ kiss; this mine.

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Patroclus kisses you.

speaker: MENELAUS
text: O, this is trim!

speaker: PATROCLUS
text: Paris and I kiss evermore for him.

speaker: MENELAUS
text: I’ll have my kiss, sir.—Lady, by your leave.

speaker: CRESSIDA
text: In kissing, do you render or receive?

speaker: MENELAUS
text: Both take and give.

speaker: CRESSIDA
text: I’ll make my match to live,

speaker: CRESSIDA
text: The kiss you take is better than you give.

speaker: CRESSIDA
text: Therefore no kiss.

speaker: MENELAUS
text: I’ll give you boot: I’ll give you three for one.

speaker: CRESSIDA
text: You are an odd man. Give even, or give none.

speaker: MENELAUS
text: An odd man, lady? Every man is odd.

speaker: CRESSIDA
text: No, Paris is not, for you know ’tis true

speaker: CRESSIDA
text: That you are odd, and he is even with you.

speaker: MENELAUS
text: You fillip me o’ th’ head.

speaker: CRESSIDA
text: No, I’ll be sworn.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: It were no match, your nail against his horn.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: May I, sweet lady, beg a kiss of you?

speaker: CRESSIDA
text: You may.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: I do desire it.

speaker: CRESSIDA
text: Why, beg two.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Why, then, for Venus’ sake, give me a kiss

speaker: ULYSSES
text: When Helen is a maid again and his.

speaker: CRESSIDA
text: I am your debtor; claim it when ’tis due.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Never’s my day, and then a kiss of you.

speaker: DIOMEDES
text: Lady, a word. I’ll bring you to your father.

speaker: NESTOR
text: A woman of quick sense.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Fie, fie upon her!

speaker: ULYSSES
text: There’s language in her eye, her cheek, her lip;

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Nay, her foot speaks. Her wanton spirits look out

speaker: ULYSSES
text: At every joint and motive of her body.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: O, these encounterers, so glib of tongue,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: That give accosting welcome ere it comes

speaker: ULYSSES
text: And wide unclasp the tables of their thoughts

speaker: ULYSSES
text: To every tickling reader! Set them down

speaker: ULYSSES
text: For sluttish spoils of opportunity

speaker: ULYSSES
text: And daughters of the game.

speaker: ALL
text: The Trojan’s trumpet.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Yonder comes the troop.

speaker: AENEAS
text: Hail, all the state of Greece! What shall be done

speaker: AENEAS
text: To him that victory commands? Or do you purpose

speaker: AENEAS
text: A victor shall be known? Will you the knights

speaker: AENEAS
text: Shall to the edge of all extremity

speaker: AENEAS
text: Pursue each other, or shall they be divided

speaker: AENEAS
text: By any voice or order of the field?

speaker: AENEAS
text: Hector bade ask.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Which way would Hector have it?

speaker: AENEAS
text: He cares not; he’ll obey conditions.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: ’Tis done like Hector.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: But securely done,

speaker: ACHILLES
text: A little proudly, and great deal misprizing

speaker: ACHILLES
text: The knight opposed.

speaker: AENEAS
text: If not Achilles, sir,

speaker: AENEAS
text: What is your name?

speaker: ACHILLES
text: If not Achilles, nothing.

speaker: AENEAS
text: Therefore Achilles. But whate’er, know this:

speaker: AENEAS
text: In the extremity of great and little,

speaker: AENEAS
text: Valor and pride excel themselves in Hector,

speaker: AENEAS
text: The one almost as infinite as all,

speaker: AENEAS
text: The other blank as nothing. Weigh him well,

speaker: AENEAS
text: And that which looks like pride is courtesy.

speaker: AENEAS
text: This Ajax is half made of Hector’s blood,

speaker: AENEAS
text: In love whereof half Hector stays at home;

speaker: AENEAS
text: Half heart, half hand, half Hector comes to seek

speaker: AENEAS
text: This blended knight, half Trojan and half Greek.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: A maiden battle, then? O, I perceive you.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Here is Sir Diomed.—Go, gentle knight;

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Stand by our Ajax. As you and Lord Aeneas

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Consent upon the order of their fight,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: So be it, either to the uttermost

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Or else a breath. The combatants being kin

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Half stints their strife before their strokes begin.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: They are opposed already.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: What Trojan is that same that looks so heavy?

speaker: ULYSSES
text: The youngest son of Priam, a true knight,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Not yet mature, yet matchless firm of word,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Speaking in deeds, and deedless in his tongue,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Not soon provoked, nor being provoked soon calmed,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: His heart and hand both open and both free.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: For what he has, he gives; what thinks, he shows;

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Yet gives he not till judgment guide his bounty,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Nor dignifies an impair thought with breath;

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Manly as Hector, but more dangerous,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: For Hector in his blaze of wrath subscribes

speaker: ULYSSES
text: To tender objects, but he in heat of action

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Is more vindicative than jealous love.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: They call him Troilus, and on him erect

speaker: ULYSSES
text: A second hope, as fairly built as Hector.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Thus says Aeneas, one that knows the youth

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Even to his inches, and with private soul

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Did in great Ilium thus translate him to me.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: They are in action.

speaker: NESTOR
text: Now, Ajax, hold thine own!

speaker: TROILUS
text: Hector, thou sleep’st. Awake thee!

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: His blows are well disposed.—There, Ajax!

speaker: DIOMEDES
text: You must no more.

speaker: AENEAS
text: Princes, enough, so please you.

speaker: AJAX
text: I am not warm yet. Let us fight again.

speaker: DIOMEDES
text: As Hector pleases.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Why, then, will I no more.—

speaker: HECTOR
text: Thou art, great lord, my father’s sister’s son,

speaker: HECTOR
text: A cousin-german to great Priam’s seed.

speaker: HECTOR
text: The obligation of our blood forbids

speaker: HECTOR
text: A gory emulation ’twixt us twain.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Were thy commixtion Greek and Trojan so

speaker: HECTOR
text: That thou couldst say “This hand is Grecian all,

speaker: HECTOR
text: And this is Trojan; the sinews of this leg

speaker: HECTOR
text: All Greek, and this all Troy; my mother’s blood

speaker: HECTOR
text: Runs on the dexter cheek, and this sinister

speaker: HECTOR
text: Bounds in my father’s,” by Jove multipotent,

speaker: HECTOR
text: Thou shouldst not bear from me a Greekish member

speaker: HECTOR
text: Wherein my sword had not impressure made

speaker: HECTOR
text: Of our rank feud. But the just gods gainsay

speaker: HECTOR
text: That any drop thou borrowd’st from thy mother,

speaker: HECTOR
text: My sacred aunt, should by my mortal sword

speaker: HECTOR
text: Be drained. Let me embrace thee, Ajax.

speaker: HECTOR
text: By him that thunders, thou hast lusty arms!

speaker: HECTOR
text: Hector would have them fall upon him thus.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Cousin, all honor to thee!

speaker: AJAX
text: I thank thee, Hector.

speaker: AJAX
text: Thou art too gentle and too free a man.

speaker: AJAX
text: I came to kill thee, cousin, and bear hence

speaker: AJAX
text: A great addition earnèd in thy death.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Not Neoptolemus so mirable—

speaker: HECTOR
text: On whose bright crest Fame with her loud’st “Oyez”

speaker: HECTOR
text: Cries “This is he”—could promise to himself

speaker: HECTOR
text: A thought of added honor torn from Hector.

speaker: AENEAS
text: There is expectance here from both the sides

speaker: AENEAS
text: What further you will do.

speaker: HECTOR
text: We’ll answer it;

speaker: HECTOR
text: The issue is embracement.—Ajax, farewell.

speaker: AJAX
text: If I might in entreaties find success,

speaker: AJAX
text: As seld I have the chance, I would desire

speaker: AJAX
text: My famous cousin to our Grecian tents.

speaker: DIOMEDES
text: ’Tis Agamemnon’s wish; and great Achilles

speaker: DIOMEDES
text: Doth long to see unarmed the valiant Hector.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Aeneas, call my brother Troilus to me,

speaker: HECTOR
text: And signify this loving interview

speaker: HECTOR
text: To the expecters of our Trojan part;

speaker: HECTOR
text: Desire them home.

speaker: HECTOR
text:
To
                 
                Ajax
                .
                 
              Give me thy hand, my cousin.

speaker: HECTOR
text: I will go eat with thee and see your knights.

speaker: AJAX
text: Great Agamemnon comes to meet us here.

speaker: HECTOR
text: The worthiest of them tell me name by name;

speaker: HECTOR
text: But for Achilles, my own searching eyes

speaker: HECTOR
text: Shall find him by his large and portly size.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Worthy all arms! As welcome as to one

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: That would be rid of such an enemy—

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: But that’s no welcome. Understand more clear:

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: What’s past and what’s to come is strewed with husks

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: And formless ruin of oblivion;

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: But in this extant moment, faith and troth,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Strained purely from all hollow bias-drawing,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Bids thee, with most divine integrity,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: From heart of very heart, great Hector, welcome.

speaker: HECTOR
text: I thank thee, most imperious Agamemnon.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: My well-famed lord of Troy, no less to you.

speaker: MENELAUS
text: Let me confirm my princely brother’s greeting:

speaker: MENELAUS
text: You brace of warlike brothers, welcome hither.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Who must we answer?

speaker: AENEAS
text: The noble Menelaus.

speaker: HECTOR
text: O, you, my lord? By Mars his gauntlet, thanks!

speaker: HECTOR
text: Mock not that I affect th’ untraded oath;

speaker: HECTOR
text: Your quondam wife swears still by Venus’ glove.

speaker: HECTOR
text: She’s well, but bade me not commend her to you.

speaker: MENELAUS
text: Name her not now, sir; she’s a deadly theme.

speaker: HECTOR
text: O, pardon! I offend.

speaker: NESTOR
text: I have, thou gallant Trojan, seen thee oft,

speaker: NESTOR
text: Laboring for destiny, make cruel way

speaker: NESTOR
text: Through ranks of Greekish youth; and I have seen thee,

speaker: NESTOR
text: As hot as Perseus, spur thy Phrygian steed,

speaker: NESTOR
text: Despising many forfeits and subduments,

speaker: NESTOR
text: When thou hast hung thy advanced sword i’ th’ air,

speaker: NESTOR
text: Not letting it decline on the declined,

speaker: NESTOR
text: That I have said to some my standers-by

speaker: NESTOR
text: “Lo, Jupiter is yonder, dealing life!”

speaker: NESTOR
text: And I have seen thee pause and take thy breath

speaker: NESTOR
text: When that a ring of Greeks have hemmed thee in,

speaker: NESTOR
text: Like an Olympian wrestling. This have I seen.

speaker: NESTOR
text: But this thy countenance, still locked in steel,

speaker: NESTOR
text: I never saw till now. I knew thy grandsire

speaker: NESTOR
text: And once fought with him; he was a soldier good,

speaker: NESTOR
text: But, by great Mars, the captain of us all,

speaker: NESTOR
text: Never like thee! O, let an old man embrace thee;

speaker: NESTOR
text: And, worthy warrior, welcome to our tents.

speaker: AENEAS
text: ’Tis the old Nestor.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Let me embrace thee, good old chronicle

speaker: HECTOR
text: That hast so long walked hand in hand with time.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Most reverend Nestor, I am glad to clasp thee.

speaker: NESTOR
text: I would my arms could match thee in contention

speaker: NESTOR
text: As they contend with thee in courtesy.

speaker: HECTOR
text: I would they could.

speaker: NESTOR
text: Ha! By this white beard, I’d fight with thee tomorrow.

speaker: NESTOR
text: Well, welcome, welcome. I have seen the time!

speaker: ULYSSES
text: I wonder now how yonder city stands

speaker: ULYSSES
text: When we have here her base and pillar by us.

speaker: HECTOR
text: I know your favor, Lord Ulysses, well.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Ah, sir, there’s many a Greek and Trojan dead

speaker: HECTOR
text: Since first I saw yourself and Diomed

speaker: HECTOR
text: In Ilium, on your Greekish embassy.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Sir, I foretold you then what would ensue.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: My prophecy is but half his journey yet,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: For yonder walls, that pertly front your town,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Yon towers, whose wanton tops do buss the clouds,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Must kiss their own feet.

speaker: HECTOR
text: I must not believe you.

speaker: HECTOR
text: There they stand yet, and modestly I think

speaker: HECTOR
text: The fall of every Phrygian stone will cost

speaker: HECTOR
text: A drop of Grecian blood. The end crowns all,

speaker: HECTOR
text: And that old common arbitrator, Time,

speaker: HECTOR
text: Will one day end it.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: So to him we leave it.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Most gentle and most valiant Hector, welcome.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: After the General, I beseech you next

speaker: ULYSSES
text: To feast with me and see me at my tent.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: I shall forestall thee, Lord Ulysses, thou!—

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Now, Hector, I have fed mine eyes on thee;

speaker: ACHILLES
text: I have with exact view perused thee, Hector,

speaker: ACHILLES
text: And quoted joint by joint.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Is this Achilles?

speaker: ACHILLES
text: I am Achilles.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Stand fair, I pray thee. Let me look on thee.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Behold thy fill.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Nay, I have done already.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Thou art too brief. I will the second time,

speaker: ACHILLES
text: As I would buy thee, view thee limb by limb.

speaker: HECTOR
text: O, like a book of sport thou ’lt read me o’er;

speaker: HECTOR
text: But there’s more in me than thou understand’st.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Why dost thou so oppress me with thine eye?

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Tell me, you heavens, in which part of his body

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Shall I destroy him—whether there, or there, or there—

speaker: ACHILLES
text: That I may give the local wound a name

speaker: ACHILLES
text: And make distinct the very breach whereout

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Hector’s great spirit flew. Answer me, heavens!

speaker: HECTOR
text: It would discredit the blest gods, proud man,

speaker: HECTOR
text: To answer such a question. Stand again.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Think’st thou to catch my life so pleasantly

speaker: HECTOR
text: As to prenominate in nice conjecture

speaker: HECTOR
text: Where thou wilt hit me dead?

speaker: ACHILLES
text: I tell thee, yea.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Wert thou an oracle to tell me so,

speaker: HECTOR
text: I’d not believe thee. Henceforth guard thee well,

speaker: HECTOR
text: For I’ll not kill thee there, nor there, nor there,

speaker: HECTOR
text: But, by the forge that stithied Mars his helm,

speaker: HECTOR
text: I’ll kill thee everywhere, yea, o’er and o’er.—

speaker: HECTOR
text: You wisest Grecians, pardon me this brag;

speaker: HECTOR
text: His insolence draws folly from my lips.

speaker: HECTOR
text: But I’ll endeavor deeds to match these words,

speaker: HECTOR
text: Or may I never—

speaker: AJAX
text: Do not chafe thee, cousin.—

speaker: AJAX
text: And you, Achilles, let these threats alone

speaker: AJAX
text: Till accident or purpose bring you to ’t.

speaker: AJAX
text: You may have every day enough of Hector

speaker: AJAX
text: If you have stomach. The general state, I fear,

speaker: AJAX
text: Can scarce entreat you to be odd with him.

speaker: HECTOR
text: I pray you, let us see you in the field.

speaker: HECTOR
text: We have had pelting wars since you refused

speaker: HECTOR
text: The Grecians’ cause.

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Dost thou entreat me, Hector?

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Tomorrow do I meet thee, fell as death;

speaker: ACHILLES
text: Tonight all friends.

speaker: HECTOR
text: Thy hand upon that match.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: First, all you peers of Greece, go to my tent;

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: There in the full convive we. Afterwards,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: As Hector’s leisure and your bounties shall

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Concur together, severally entreat him.

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: Beat loud the taborins; let the trumpets blow,

speaker: AGAMEMNON
text: That this great soldier may his welcome know.

speaker: TROILUS
text: My Lord Ulysses, tell me, I beseech you,

speaker: TROILUS
text: In what place of the field doth Calchas keep?

speaker: ULYSSES
text: At Menelaus’ tent, most princely Troilus.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: There Diomed doth feast with him tonight,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: Who neither looks upon the heaven nor Earth,

speaker: ULYSSES
text: But gives all gaze and bent of amorous view

speaker: ULYSSES
text: On the fair Cressid.

speaker: TROILUS
text: Shall I, sweet lord, be bound to you so much,

speaker: TROILUS
text: After we part from Agamemnon’s tent,

speaker: TROILUS
text: To bring me thither?

speaker: ULYSSES
text: You shall command me, sir.

speaker: ULYSSES
text: As gentle tell me, of what honor was

speaker: ULYSSES
text: This Cressida in Troy? Had she no lover there

speaker: ULYSSES
text: That wails her absence?

speaker: TROILUS
text: O sir, to such as boasting show their scars

speaker: TROILUS
text: A mock is due. Will you walk on, my lord?

speaker: TROILUS
text: She was beloved, she loved; she is, and doth;

speaker: TROILUS
text: But still sweet love is food for Fortune’s tooth.
